_meta.description: Copy tiers for teacher benchmark results. All band keys are evaluated top-to-bottom — first match wins. Scores are readiness percentages (0-100) unless noted.

emerging.label: Emerging
emerging.consequence: Your AI practice has significant gaps. Focus on building safer habits before expanding your use of AI tools in the classroom.
emerging.urgent_action: Review your data protection habits and speak to your DPO before using AI tools with pupil data.

developing.label: Developing
developing.consequence: You have foundational awareness but some areas need strengthening. Targeted improvement in your weakest domains will make a meaningful difference.
developing.urgent_action: Address your two lowest-scoring domains before the next term.

established.label: Established
established.consequence: You are demonstrating responsible AI practice in most areas. Focus now on the domains where your habits are still inconsistent.

strong.label: Strong
strong.consequence: You are demonstrating good AI governance behaviours — sustain consistency as tools evolve.

leading.label: Leading
leading.consequence: You are demonstrating exemplary AI practice. You are well placed to support colleagues and contribute to school AI policy.

exemplary.consequence: You are reviewing and adapting almost all AI output before use. This is the standard all teachers should aim for — share your approach with colleagues.

strong.consequence: Share of AI output reviewed or changed before use. Below 26% signals reliance without meaningful human review.

moderate.consequence: You are reviewing most AI output but some content is going unchecked. Aim to review everything before it reaches pupils.

low.consequence: A significant portion of AI output is going unreviewed. Inconsistent checking means errors are likely reaching pupils or colleagues.

critical.consequence: Most AI output is going directly to pupils without meaningful review. Errors, bias or inappropriate content are likely reaching your classroom unchecked.

minimal.consequence: AI is used purposefully and as a complement to your own judgement, not a replacement.

low.consequence: You are generally attempting tasks independently before using AI. Keep this habit as tools become more capable.

moderate.consequence: Build habits of independent work before reaching for AI tools.

high.consequence: AI is becoming a first resort in several areas. Build habits of independent thinking before reaching for AI tools.

critical.consequence: You are frequently reaching for AI before attempting tasks independently. This limits your professional judgement and pupils may be modelling the same habit.

strong.consequence: You actively consider whether AI outputs could be unfair or discriminatory before using them with pupils.

developing.consequence: Some awareness of AI bias exists but it is not consistently applied. Build a habit of asking whether AI outputs could disadvantage particular groups of pupils.

low.consequence: AI tools can reflect and reinforce bias against pupils based on race, gender, SEND status and other characteristics. This is a safeguarding and equality duty concern.

critical.consequence: Your school has not yet assessed whether AI tools could produce unfair or discriminatory outputs affecting pupils. This is a safeguarding and equality duty risk.

headline.emerging: Your AI habits need urgent attention. Start with safer checks before expanding classroom use.
headline.developing: Your classroom AI habits are forming, but verification and safer routines need to become consistent.
headline.established: Your classroom AI habits are useful, but verification needs to become routine.
headline.strong: Your responsible AI habits are strong. Keep refining the areas where pupils rely on your judgement most.
headline.leading: You are modelling strong responsible AI practice and can help colleagues build safer habits.

safe_adoption.label: Safe adoption
safe_adoption.description: Whether AI tools are assessed for appropriateness and safety before use with pupils or colleagues.

critical.summary: AI tools are being used without a safety assessment. Before using any AI tool with pupils, assess it against your school's approved list and DfE guidance.
impact: Unapproved tools potentially processing pupil data
No consistent standard for which tools are classroom-safe
Risk of pupils encountering harmful or inappropriate AI output
actions: Check every AI tool against your school's approved list before use
If no approved list exists, raise this with your line manager or AI lead
Do not introduce new AI tools to pupils without prior approval

high.summary: Tool assessment is inconsistent. Some AI tools enter classroom use without a full safety check.
actions: Adopt the habit of checking tools before use, not after
Ask your school AI lead for the current approved tool list
Flag any tools you're unsure about rather than assuming they're safe

moderate.summary: Most tools are assessed but some slip through without a full check.
actions: Make tool approval a non-negotiable step before classroom introduction
Confirm your school's approved list is current

human_oversight.label: Human oversight
human_oversight.description: How consistently AI outputs are checked, cross-referenced and modified before reaching pupils.

critical.summary: Most AI output is going directly to pupils or colleagues without meaningful review. Errors, bias or inappropriate content are likely reaching your classroom unchecked.
impact: AI errors presented to pupils as fact
Biased or inappropriate content not caught before classroom use
No professional judgement applied between AI and pupil
actions: Review every piece of AI-generated content before sharing it with pupils
Use the Verify Before You Trust framework as a daily habit
Flag any AI errors you catch to colleagues so the whole department learns

high.summary: A significant portion of AI output is going unreviewed. Inconsistent checking means errors are likely reaching pupils or colleagues.
actions: Set a personal rule — nothing AI-generated goes to pupils without at least one read-through
Cross-reference AI factual claims with a trusted source
Modify AI output so it reflects your own voice and judgement

moderate.summary: You are reviewing most AI output but some content is going unchecked.
actions: Build a habit of reading all AI output before sharing
Pay particular attention to factual claims and pupil-specific content
Use the Verify Before You Trust checklist as a quick sense-check

independent_practice.label: Independent practice
independent_practice.description: How often you attempt tasks without AI first, and your ability to work effectively without AI support.

critical.summary: You are frequently using AI before attempting tasks independently. This limits your professional judgement and may be modelling unhealthy AI habits for pupils.
impact: Core tasks being delegated to AI rather than attempted first
Reduced ability to work confidently without AI support
Risk of over-reliance spreading to pupil practice
actions: Attempt all planning and marking tasks independently before using AI
Use AI to refine your first draft, not to produce it
Set one AI-free day per week to rebuild independent habits

high.summary: AI is becoming a first resort in some areas. Build habits of independent work before reaching for AI tools.
actions: Practise core tasks without AI first — use it to refine, not to originate
Notice which tasks you reach for AI on immediately and attempt those first independently
Track your AI-free output over a fortnight

moderate.summary: Independent practice is reasonable but inconsistent.
actions: Practise core tasks without AI first — use it to refine, not to originate
Identify the two or three tasks you most rely on AI for and build independence there first

privacy_data_protection.label: Privacy & data protection
privacy_data_protection.description: Whether identifiable pupil data, SEND information or safeguarding details are kept out of public AI tools.

critical.summary: Identifiable pupil data may be entering public AI tools. This is a UK GDPR risk and a safeguarding concern.
impact: Pupil names or details entered into ChatGPT or similar tools
SEND or safeguarding information potentially stored on third-party servers
No clear personal rule for what data is and is not safe to enter
actions: Stop entering any identifiable pupil data into public AI tools immediately
Check your school's approved tool list and use only those
Ask your DPO what data handling rules apply to AI tools you currently use

high.summary: Data protection awareness is inconsistent. Some pupil information may be entering AI tools without certainty it is safe.
actions: Never enter identifiable pupil, SEND or safeguarding data into public AI tools
Review which tools you use against your school's approved list
If unsure whether a tool is safe, ask your DPO before using it

moderate.summary: You have reasonable data awareness but some uncertainty remains about what is and is not safe.
actions: Never enter identifiable pupil, SEND or safeguarding data into public AI tools
Confirm your school's approved tool list is up to date and follow it consistently

safeguarding.label: Safeguarding
safeguarding.description: Awareness of AI-enabled harms — deepfakes, impersonation and synthetic content — and how to respond.

critical.summary: You may not be aware of AI-specific safeguarding risks. These are now part of the online safety landscape all teachers need to understand.
impact: Unable to recognise AI-enabled harm disclosures from pupils
No knowledge of reporting process for deepfake or impersonation incidents
Pupils may be experiencing AI-enabled harm without teacher awareness
actions: Complete your school's online safety training if it covers AI scenarios
Speak to your DSL about what to do if a pupil reports a deepfake incident
Know the signs that a pupil may be experiencing AI-enabled harm

high.summary: General online safety awareness is in place but AI-specific scenarios may not yet be fully understood.
actions: Familiarise yourself with deepfake and AI impersonation risks
Know your school's reporting process for AI-related safeguarding concerns
Attend any available DSL-led briefing on AI and online safety

moderate.summary: Core AI safeguarding awareness is in place. Keep pace as new AI-enabled harms emerge.
actions: Stay current with KCSIE updates covering AI-enabled harm
Confirm all pastoral staff have received AI scenario training

assessment_integrity.label: Assessment integrity
assessment_integrity.description: Understanding of JCQ and school rules around AI use in assessed work, and consistent application of those rules.

critical.summary: AI rules in assessed work may not be clearly understood or consistently applied. Pupils may be unclear on what is and is not acceptable.
impact: Inconsistent AI rules communicated across classes
Risk of malpractice incidents due to unclear boundaries
Potential JCQ compliance gap
actions: Read your school's current AI policy on assessed work
Communicate AI boundaries clearly to all classes before the next assessment
Check your practice against current JCQ guidance

high.summary: Assessment boundaries are understood in principle but not always applied consistently.
actions: Clarify your school's AI rules for all assessed work you set
Brief pupils explicitly on what AI use is and is not permitted in your subject
Update your malpractice awareness in line with current JCQ guidance

moderate.summary: Assessment integrity is reasonable but some inconsistency remains in how AI rules are communicated.
actions: Make AI boundaries explicit at the start of every assessed task
Ensure your practice is consistent with your department's approach
Stay current with JCQ guidance updates

ai_literacy.label: AI literacy
ai_literacy.description: Understanding of how AI tools work, where they fail, and what their outputs can and cannot be trusted for.

critical.summary: Understanding of how AI tools work and where they fail appears limited. Without this foundation, safe and effective AI use is difficult to sustain.
impact: Unable to recognise AI hallucination or confident errors
No basis for judging when AI output can and cannot be trusted
Risk of modelling uncritical AI use to pupils
actions: Complete a short AI literacy course this term
Read your school's AI guidance if available
Explore how large language models work and where they commonly produce errors

high.summary: You have basic AI awareness but gaps in understanding how tools fail, hallucinate or reflect bias.
actions: Learn the common failure modes of AI tools — hallucination, bias, confident errors
Apply this knowledge when verifying AI outputs
Share what you learn with colleagues

moderate.summary: AI literacy is reasonable. Deepen your understanding of bias and failure modes.
actions: Read about how AI bias affects educational contexts specifically
Test AI tools on edge cases to see where they fail
Discuss AI limitations openly with pupils

bias_awareness.label: Bias awareness
bias_awareness.description: Whether AI outputs are checked for potential bias or unfairness before use with pupils.

critical.summary: AI tools can reflect and reinforce bias against pupils based on race, gender, SEND status and other characteristics. This has not yet been addressed in your practice.
impact: AI outputs potentially disadvantaging particular groups of pupils
No check in place before biased content reaches the classroom
Potential breach of Public Sector Equality Duty
actions: Before using AI output with pupils, ask: could this disadvantage any group?
Flag any AI output that describes different groups unevenly or unfairly
Raise AI bias as a topic in your next department or safeguarding meeting

high.summary: Some awareness of AI bias exists but it is not consistently applied before classroom use.
actions: Build a habit of checking AI output for bias before sharing with pupils
Pay particular attention to AI outputs about people, history and social topics
Ask your school AI lead whether bias checking is part of your AI policy

moderate.summary: Bias awareness is developing. Make it a consistent part of your verification habit.
actions: Add a bias check to your existing Verify Before You Trust process
Discuss AI bias with pupils as part of AI literacy lessons

bias_equality.label: Bias & equality
bias_equality.description: Whether AI outputs are checked for potential bias, stereotypes or unfairness before use with pupils.

critical.summary: AI tools can reflect and reinforce bias against pupils based on race, gender, SEND status and other characteristics. This has not yet been addressed in your practice.
impact: AI outputs potentially disadvantaging particular groups of pupils
No check in place before biased content reaches the classroom
Potential breach of Public Sector Equality Duty
actions: Before using AI output with pupils, ask: could this disadvantage any group?
Flag any AI output that describes different groups unevenly or unfairly
Raise AI bias as a topic in your next department or safeguarding meeting

high.summary: AI outputs can be unfair or discriminatory. Build a simple bias spot-check into your verify-before-use routine.
actions: Check AI examples for protected characteristics and stereotypes
Add bias review to your normal output verification habit
Use one lesson example to show pupils how AI can be unfair

moderate.summary: Bias awareness is developing. Make it a consistent part of your verification habit.
actions: Add a bias check to your existing Verify Before You Trust process
Discuss AI bias with pupils as part of AI literacy lessons

strengths.safe_adoption_100: Thoughtful assessment of AI tools before use — safe adoption 100%.
strengths.safe_adoption_76: You assess AI tools before introducing them — a habit many teachers skip.
strengths.human_oversight_90: Exemplary oversight — you review and adapt almost all AI output before use.
strengths.human_oversight_76: Strong human oversight of AI outputs — above the threshold that signals meaningful review.
strengths.independent_practice_76: Consistent independent thinking before reaching for AI — your professional judgement leads.
strengths.privacy_76: Strong awareness of pupil data risks in AI tools.
strengths.safeguarding_76: Good awareness of AI-enabled safeguarding risks.
strengths.assessment_integrity_100: Clear understanding of AI rules in assessed work.
strengths.ai_literacy_76: Strong understanding of AI limitations and appropriate use.
strengths.bias_awareness_76: You actively consider whether AI outputs could be unfair to particular groups of pupils.

emerging.title: AI awareness session
emerging.description: Build the foundational knowledge and habits your practice needs. A focused session covering safe adoption, verification and data protection basics.

developing.title: Targeted CPD on your priority domains
developing.description: Your results show specific gaps. A targeted CPD session will address your two or three weakest domains with practical classroom strategies.

established.title: Classroom resources aligned to your results
established.description: Your practice is solid — supplement it with resources matched to your audit. Lesson activities, prompts and materials aligned to your priority domains.

strong.title: AI Champion pathway
strong.description: You demonstrate strong AI awareness and responsible practice. You may be well placed to support colleagues, shape school policy and lead from within.

leading.title: Lead and share your practice
leading.description: Your results place you among the strongest AI practitioners in the sector. Share your approach, support colleagues and contribute to the national benchmark.
